narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in some positive ways, but the admissions model focused heavily on predictors of bar exam success. The model assessed your overall application and predicted a 90.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which directly led to the rejection of your university admission since the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in practice. This prediction wasn't a judgment on your potential or character, but a data-driven estimate based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic records and outcomes. The three biggest factors pushing the model toward this "fail" prediction were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 school decile ranking, in that order of influence. Your Year 3 decile of 1—far below the typical student's 5.5—had the strongest negative pull, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance placed you in the bottom tier compared to peers who generally pass the bar. Similarly, your LSAT score of 25.5, well under the average of 36.7, reinforced concerns about readiness for the rigors ahead, as higher scores strongly correlate with first-time bar passage in the data. Even your Year 1 decile of 1, again much lower than the average around 5.7, added to this by showing early academic struggles that the model links to higher failure risk. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 was close to the average of 3.21 and your full-time status aligns with most successful students, these weren't enough to offset the top concerns. Compared to typical admitted students, who often have mid-to-high deciles and stronger LSATs, your metrics aligned more closely with those who faced bar challenges. This isn't the end of your path—many students in similar positions improve dramatically with targeted prep. I recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score, seeking academic advising to understand and address what held back your decile rankings, and perhaps gaining practical experience like internships to build a stronger narrative. Reapplying after these steps could shift the model's view in your favor, opening doors to law school and bar success.
